Intermediaries 
Date 
January 12, 1978 
This is in response to your memorandum dated 
October 6, 1977, concerning intermediaries. 
I apologize 
for the delay in responding. 
Government Code Section 84302 requires· that a 
person who makes a contribution on behalf of another, 
or while acting as an agent or intermediary of another, 
disclose to the recipient of the contribution the name 
of the person on whose behalf the agent or intermediary 
is acting. 
The Act also requires that identification 
of both the intermediary and the contributor of a con-
tribution ~, included on the recipient's campaign 
statement.-
The principal purpose of the requirement is 
to permit the tracing of funds so the true source of a 
contribution can be verified. 
It is the staff's view, 
therefore, that a person acts as an intermediary only 
in those cases w~,re the true source of a contribution 
may be confused.-
For example, an individual who acts 
merely as a messenger by delivering a contribution from 
a donor to a recipient is not required to be disclosed 
as an intermediary on the recipient's campaign statement. 
In addition, if a check is made out to B but is earmarked 
by the donor, A, to be given to a specific candidate 
and B endorses the check over to the candidate, B is 
not an intermediary. 
The face of the check received 
]/ Note 
t an "agent" does not 
e to be 
disc 
, onI 
an inte 
iary. 
~/ This view is limited to intermediaries for 
purposes of Chapter 4 of the Political Reform Act. 


Robert C. Summers 
January 12, 1978 
Page Two 
by the candidate discloses A as the true source of the 
contribution. 
B has been given specific instructions 
and has no discretion over the making of the contribution. 
However, once there may be confusion about 
the source of the contribution, the person delivering 
the contribution becomes an intermediary. 
Accordingly, 
if A gives B money for a contribution to a specific 
candidate and B writes a separate check for that amount 
payable to the candidate, B must be reported on the 
candidate's campaign statement as the intermediary of 
A's contribution. 
If B is given money for campaign contributions 
and is given discretion to decide how large the contribu-
tions shall be or who the recipients shall be, he or 
is acting as a committee rather than an intermediary. 
B would therefore have to file as a committee under 
Government Code Section 82013(a) once $500 was received. 
Applying these principles to the situations 
you posed, none of the people who delivered the checks 
are intermediaries for purposes of disclosure. 
In situation 1, this conclusion is based on 
the assumption that B, in "actually handl[ing)" the 
funds, only gives the check to A which C prepares for A. 
In that situation B is only acting as C's messenger. 
If 
C were to earmark a check for candidate A but make it 
payable to Band B simply endorsed the check over to A, 
B would also not be an intermediary. 
In situations 2-5, the people who actually 
deliver the checks are only acting in a messenger capacity. 
They would not have to be disclosed as intermediaries on 
the recipient's campaign statement. 
This conclusion as 
to situations 3, 4 and 5 assumes that the association or 
committee making the contribution prepared the check. 
If you have any questions, please feel free to

sections.question: Whether persons delivering contributions on behalf of others or while acting as agents/intermediaries must be disclosed on campaign statements, with specific application to five hypothetical situations involving contribution delivery mechanisms.
sections.conclusion: None of the people who delivered the checks in the situations posed are intermediaries for purposes of disclosure. In situation 1, B is only acting as a messenger. In situations 2-5, the people who actually deliver checks are only acting in a messenger capacity and would not have to be disclosed as intermediaries on the recipient's campaign statement.
sections.question_synthetic: Does California Political Reform Act Section 84302 require disclosure of intermediaries and agents who deliver contributions, and when do messengers versus true intermediaries require disclosure?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: FPPC staff concluded that persons acting merely as messengers delivering contributions do not require disclosure as intermediaries, but true intermediaries with discretion over contribution amounts or recipients must be disclosed. The test is whether confusion about the true source of the contribution may arise.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document quality is poor with significant OCR errors and text corruption throughout. The incoming request section appears to describe 5 hypothetical situations but text is heavily damaged. The response/conclusion sections are more legible. Confidence is reduced due to document degradation and incomplete reproduction of the original request. Core legal analysis appears intact.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether persons delivering contributions on behalf of others or while acting as agents/intermediaries must be disclosed on campaign statements, with specific application to five hypothetical situations involving contribution delivery mechanisms.

CONCLUSION: None of the people who delivered the checks in the situations posed are intermediaries for purposes of disclosure. In situation 1, B is only acting as a messenger. In situations 2-5, the people who actually deliver checks are only acting in a messenger capacity and would not have to be disclosed as intermediaries on the recipient's campaign statement.

embedding.summary: FPPC response to Franchise Tax Board regarding disclosure requirements for intermediaries under Government Code Section 84302. The letter establishes that intermediary disclosure is required only when the true source of a contribution may be confused, distinguishing between mere messengers (no disclosure) and true intermediaries (disclosure required).
